A2.title: How coral reefs are formed
A2.body:
Have you ever seen a coral reef? It is a beautiful underwater garden, full of color. But how does it grow?

Coral may look like a plant or a rock, but it is actually an animal. A coral animal is very small. Many small animals live together. They build hard shells around their bodies. Slowly, the shells grow. Over many, many years, they form a reef.

Coral needs sun and warm water. So most reefs grow in warm seas. They also need clean water. Today, the water is too warm in many places. Sadly, some reefs are dying. We must protect them.

B1.title: How coral reefs are formed
B1.body:
Have you ever seen a coral reef in a photo or a video? It looks like a beautiful underwater garden, full of bright colors. But how does something like this actually grow?

A coral might look like a plant or a strange rock, but in reality it is an animal. Each coral is a tiny creature called a polyp. A single polyp is very small, but thousands or even millions of them live together in one place. Each polyp builds a hard shell of calcium around its soft body for protection.

Here is the interesting part. When a polyp dies, its hard shell stays behind. New polyps grow on top of the old shells. After hundreds and thousands of years, layers and layers of shells form a huge structure under the sea — a coral reef.

Coral needs warm sea water, plenty of sunlight, and clean water to grow. That is why most reefs are found in warm, shallow oceans near the equator. Sadly, in many places the water has become too warm, and reefs are slowly dying. So this slow, beautiful work of millions of tiny animals is now in real danger.

key_phrases.phrase: after hundreds and thousands of years

B2.title: How coral reefs are formed
B2.body:
Have you ever seen a coral reef in a documentary and wondered how something so vast and colorful could grow on the ocean floor? It looks like an underwater garden, but the answer is actually stranger and more interesting.

A coral might look like a plant or an oddly shaped rock, but in reality each one is an animal — a tiny, soft-bodied creature called a polyp. A single polyp is no larger than a grain of rice, but thousands or even millions of them live together in colonies. Each polyp slowly builds a hard skeleton of calcium carbonate around its soft body, mainly for protection from predators and waves.

The remarkable part is what happens over time. When an individual polyp dies, its hard skeleton remains. New polyps grow on top of those abandoned skeletons, and then more grow on top of them. Over hundreds, thousands, and even millions of years, layer upon layer of these tiny skeletons accumulate into the enormous reef structures we see today. In other words, a coral reef is essentially a vast, ancient apartment building constructed by countless generations of tiny animals.

This entire process is surprisingly fragile. Reef-building corals depend on warm, shallow water, abundant sunlight, and clean conditions, which is why almost all major reefs are found in tropical seas near the equator. They also rely on a partnership with microscopic algae that live inside their tissues and provide them with food.

Unfortunately, when sea temperatures rise even slightly, this delicate partnership breaks down. The algae are expelled, the coral loses its color in an event known as bleaching, and if conditions do not improve quickly, the polyps starve and die. The patient work of millions of years can be undone in just a few warm summers.

C1.title: How coral reefs are formed
C1.body:
Have you ever watched a documentary about a coral reef and quietly wondered how something so vast and intricately colorful could possibly assemble itself on the ocean floor? At first glance, a reef looks like an enormous underwater garden, but the truth is rather more surprising — and arguably more impressive — than that.

A coral, despite appearances, is neither a plant nor a curiously shaped stone. Each one is in fact an animal — a tiny, soft-bodied creature called a polyp, no larger than a grain of rice. Polyps almost never live alone. Instead, thousands or even millions of genetically identical individuals cluster together in colonies, each one secreting a hard skeleton of calcium carbonate around its delicate body, principally as defense against predators and the constant motion of the sea.

The truly remarkable process unfolds over deep time. When an individual polyp dies, its mineral skeleton is left behind. The next generation of polyps takes root on top of those abandoned skeletons, builds its own layer, and is itself replaced. Over thousands and even millions of years, these accumulated layers compound into the immense reef structures we see today. In a sense, a coral reef is best understood as a vast, ancient apartment building patiently constructed by countless generations of tiny architects, each contributing a single brick to a project none of them could ever see completed.

This entire architectural feat depends on a startlingly narrow set of conditions. Reef-building corals require warm, shallow water, generous sunlight, and remarkable clarity, which is why nearly every major reef on Earth is concentrated in a tropical band near the equator. Even more crucially, they depend on a microscopic partner — photosynthetic algae called zooxanthellae — which live embedded in their tissues and supply most of the energy the polyps need to survive and build.

This partnership is also their greatest vulnerability. When sea temperatures climb even a couple of degrees above normal for too long, the relationship collapses. The corals expel their algae, lose their color in a phenomenon known as bleaching, and unless cooler conditions return quickly, slowly starve. The quiet labour of millions of years can be undone in the span of a few unusually warm summers — a sobering reminder that some of the planet's most spectacular structures rest on partnerships almost too small to see.

key_phrases.meaning_ko: 눈에 거의 보이지 않을 만큼 작은

A2.title: How writing was invented
A2.body:
Long ago, people did not have writing. They only used their voice. They told stories to remember things. But voice is not perfect. People forget. People die.

So one day, in a place called Sumer, people had a new idea. They made small marks on wet clay. The marks were like simple pictures. Each picture was a thing — a cow, a fish, a man.

This was the start of writing. It was about five thousand years ago. With writing, people could keep numbers. They could send a message far away. They could save knowledge. Writing changed the world forever.

B1.title: How writing was invented
B1.body:
For most of human history, people did not have writing. To remember important things, they only had their voice. They told stories around the fire, and they passed knowledge from parents to children. But voice has a big problem. People forget. People die. And every time a story is told again, it changes a little.

About five thousand years ago, in an ancient land called Sumer, people came up with a brilliant solution. They started pressing simple marks into wet clay with a small stick. At first, each mark was just a tiny picture of a real thing — a cow, a fish, a person.

Slowly, these pictures became more abstract. They started to stand for sounds, not just objects. This was the moment writing was truly born.

Imagine the impact of this small invention. Now, for the first time, a king could send a message to a city far away. A farmer could record exactly how much wheat he had. Stories did not die when storytellers died. In a real sense, the modern world began on those small pieces of wet clay.

B2.title: How writing was invented
B2.body:
For most of human history, people had no writing at all. To remember anything that mattered — laws, trade agreements, family histories — they had only their voice. Stories were told around fires and passed from parents to children, generation after generation. But human memory has a stubborn problem. People forget. People die. And every time a story is retold, it quietly drifts a little from the version before.

Around five thousand years ago, in an ancient region called Sumer, in what is now southern Iraq, people came up with a remarkably practical solution. They began pressing small marks into pieces of wet clay using a sharpened reed. In its earliest form, the system was straightforward: each mark was a simple picture of a real thing — a cow, a basket of grain, a person.

Over the centuries, however, something interesting happened. The pictures became more abstract and stylized, and crucially, some of them started to represent sounds rather than objects. This shift, from picture to sound, is what scholars consider the true birth of writing.

It is hard to overstate the impact of this quiet invention. For the first time, a king could send precise instructions to a distant city without trusting a messenger's memory. A farmer could record exactly how much wheat he had stored. A merchant could prove what he was owed. And perhaps most importantly, knowledge stopped dying when the people who held it died. In a real sense, the long arc of the modern world — its laws, its science, its bureaucracies — began on those small, unimpressive pieces of wet clay.

C1.title: How writing was invented
C1.body:
For most of the long history of our species, human beings had no writing whatsoever. To preserve anything that mattered — laws, treaties, family lineages, trade arrangements — people relied almost entirely on the spoken word. Knowledge was carried in memory, performed around fires, and handed down from parents to children, generation after generation. But human memory has an old, stubborn problem. People forget. People die. And every time a story is retold, it quietly drifts a little from the version that came before, until eventually no one can be sure what the original even looked like.

Roughly five thousand years ago, in an ancient region called Sumer — located in what is today southern Iraq — people arrived at a remarkably practical solution. They began pressing small marks into pieces of wet clay using a sharpened reed, then drying or baking the clay until it became almost as durable as stone. In its earliest form, the system was essentially pictorial: each mark stood for a tangible object — a cow, a basket of grain, a person — and a record was little more than a small visual inventory.

Over the following centuries, however, something subtle and profound began to happen. The pictures gradually became more abstract and stylized, and, crucially, some of them began to stand not for objects but for sounds. This single conceptual leap — from depicting things to encoding the noises of speech itself — is what most scholars regard as the true birth of writing.

It is genuinely difficult to overstate the consequences of this quiet invention. For the first time in human history, a king could send precise instructions to a distant city without depending on a messenger's fragile memory. A farmer could record, beyond dispute, exactly how much grain had been stored and how much was owed. A merchant could prove the terms of a deal that had taken place years before. And perhaps most importantly, knowledge stopped vanishing along with the people who held it. A thought set down in clay could outlive its author by thousands of years.

In a very real sense, the long arc of what we now call the modern world — its codified laws, its accumulated sciences, its sprawling bureaucracies, even its literature — first began to be drawn on those small, unimpressive pieces of wet Sumerian clay. It is worth pausing on that occasionally: nearly everything we treat as permanent today rests on someone, long ago, deciding that a fleeting human thought deserved to be pressed into mud.

key_phrases.phrase: arrived at a remarkably practical solution

key_phrases.meaning_ko: 잠시 멈추고 생각해 볼 가치가 있다

topics.title_ko: 해시 테이블 조회가 평균 O(1)인 이유

A2.title: Why hash table lookup is O(1) on average
A2.body:
Imagine a very big book. The book has names and phone numbers. You want to find one name fast. If the book has no order, you must read every page. That is slow.

But what if there is a trick? You take the name, and a machine gives you a number. The number is the page. You go straight to that page. You find the name fast.

This is the simple idea of a hash table. Hash means a special number for each name. With a hash, your computer can find data very quickly. That is why hash tables are used everywhere.

B1.title: Why hash table lookup is O(1) on average
B1.body:
Imagine you have a giant phone book with millions of names. You need to find one person, fast. If the names are in no order at all, you have to read every page. That is painfully slow.

A hash table is a clever way to solve this problem. The idea is simple. You have a name, and a small piece of math — called a hash function — turns that name into a number. That number tells you exactly which slot in memory to look at. Instead of searching, you jump straight to the right place.

This is why people say a hash table is O(1) on average. O(1) just means the work does not grow when your data grows. If you have ten million names, you still do roughly the same amount of work to find one.

There is a catch, of course. Sometimes two different names get the same hash number. That is called a collision, and hash tables have clever ways to handle it. But on average, when the design is good, you really do get one-jump lookup. That tiny trick is everywhere in software.

key_phrases.phrase: there is a catch, of course

B2.title: Why hash table lookup is O(1) on average
B2.body:
Imagine you have a phone book with ten million names, and you need to find one particular person as quickly as possible. If the names are in no order at all, your only option is to check every page one by one. As the book grows, the work grows with it, and very soon the whole approach becomes unusable.

A hash table is a clever way around this problem, and it is one of the most quietly useful ideas in all of computer science. The trick is this. You have a key — say, a name — and you apply a small mathematical function to it called a hash function. The hash function takes your key and turns it into a number, which is then used to point directly to one specific slot in memory. Instead of searching, you simply jump to that slot.

This is what people mean when they say a hash table offers O(1) average-time lookup. O(1) is shorthand for the fact that the work does not grow as your data grows. Whether the table holds one hundred names or one hundred million, the time to find any single name stays roughly constant.

There is, of course, a catch. Sometimes two completely different keys end up producing the same hash number — an unavoidable situation called a collision. Hash tables have several well-known strategies for dealing with collisions, such as storing colliding items in a small list at each slot, but if a hash function is poor and collisions become very frequent, that beautiful constant-time behavior degrades quickly.

In practice, with a well-designed hash function and a sensible load factor, the average case really does deliver near-instant lookups. That is why hash tables sit quietly underneath almost every piece of modern software, from databases to compilers to your browser's address bar.

key_phrases.phrase: a clever way around this problem

C1.title: Why hash table lookup is O(1) on average
C1.body:
Imagine you have a phone book containing ten million names, and you need to locate one particular person as quickly as possible. If the names are arranged in no order whatsoever, your only honest option is to scan the entire book, one page at a time. The amount of work you do grows linearly with the size of the data, and very soon the approach becomes hopelessly impractical.

A hash table is one of the quietly brilliant ideas of computer science, and it offers a way around this problem that is, in a certain sense, almost magical the first time you see it. The mechanism is conceptually simple. You take a key — perhaps a name, an integer, or a string — and pass it through a small mathematical function known as a hash function. That function deterministically transforms your key into a fixed-size integer, which then points directly to a specific bucket or slot in an underlying array. Instead of searching the structure, you compute an address and jump straight to it.

This is precisely what is meant when people describe a hash table as offering O(1) average-time lookup. O(1) is shorthand for the property that the cost of an operation does not grow as the dataset grows. Whether the table contains a hundred items or a hundred million, the average time required to find, insert, or remove any single key remains essentially constant. That is an extraordinary guarantee, and one that, for most realistic workloads, holds up remarkably well in practice.

There is, of course, a catch lurking behind the elegance. Because a hash function maps a potentially infinite space of keys into a finite array of slots, two genuinely different keys will sometimes produce the same hash value — a situation known as a collision. Hash tables employ a small family of well-studied techniques to deal with collisions — separate chaining, open addressing, and several elaborations on each — but if the hash function distributes keys poorly or the table becomes too crowded, the beautiful constant-time behavior degrades, in the worst case, all the way back to linear search.

In a well-engineered hash table, with a thoughtful hash function and a sensible load factor, the average case continues to deliver something very close to instant lookup. That quiet guarantee is exactly why hash tables sit invisibly underneath an enormous fraction of modern software, from databases and compilers to network routers and the address bar of every browser you have ever opened.

key_phrases.phrase: almost magical the first time you see it
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 처음 봤을 때 거의 마법 같은

key_phrases.phrase: is precisely what is meant when
with a thoughtful X and a sensible Y
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 신중한 X와 적절한 Y를 갖춘 채

A2.title: What is consistent hashing
A2.body:
Imagine a small office with one mailbox for letters. Many letters come every day. Soon, the mailbox is full. So you buy more mailboxes.

Now, where does each letter go? One way is: take the first letter of the name, and use that. But what if you add or remove a mailbox? Then everything changes. You must move all the letters. That is bad.

There is a smarter way. We use a special trick called consistent hashing. With this trick, when you add or remove one mailbox, only a few letters need to move. The rest stay in place. It saves a lot of work.

B1.title: What is consistent hashing
B1.body:
Imagine a busy online service with millions of users. Their data has to live on many computers, called servers, because one server is not enough. A simple way to assign data is to take the user's ID, calculate a number, and use 'number modulo number of servers' to decide which server stores their data.

This sounds fine, until the day you need to add or remove a server. Suddenly the modulo is different for everyone. Almost every user's data has to move to a new server. The whole system shakes.

Consistent hashing is a clever idea that solves this problem. The trick is to put the servers on an imaginary circle, and use a hash to place each piece of data on the same circle. Each piece of data is owned by the next server going clockwise.

The beautiful part is what happens when a server is added or removed. Only a small slice of the circle changes hands. The rest of the data stays exactly where it was. That is why most large distributed systems quietly use this idea under the hood.

B2.title: What is consistent hashing
B2.body:
Imagine you are running a busy web service with millions of users, and you have decided to spread their data across, say, ten servers, because one machine is simply not enough. The most obvious way to decide which server stores which user's data is to hash the user ID and take the result modulo ten. Whichever number falls out is the server you use.

This approach works beautifully — until the moment you need to add an eleventh server. Suddenly the modulo changes for almost every user, which means almost every piece of data has to be moved to a new home. The system has to shuffle nearly its entire state just to accommodate one new machine, and the same thing happens whenever a server is removed.

Consistent hashing is an elegant solution to this problem, and it is one of the foundational ideas of modern distributed systems. The intuition is surprisingly visual. You imagine a circle representing the entire range of hash values. Each server is placed at one or more positions on this circle, and each piece of data is also hashed onto a position on the same circle. The rule is simple: each piece of data belongs to the next server you encounter when walking clockwise from its position.

The crucial property — and the reason this idea matters — is how the system behaves when servers come and go. When you add a server, only the small slice of the circle immediately before the new server changes hands. When you remove a server, only the data that previously belonged to it has to be moved, and it simply slides to its clockwise neighbor. The vast majority of the data stays exactly where it was.

That quiet stability is what allows large caches, key-value stores, and content delivery networks to grow, shrink, and survive failures without rebalancing nearly everything every time something changes.

key_phrases.meaning_ko: 왔다 갔다 하다, 들어왔다 나갔다 하다

C1.title: What is consistent hashing
C1.body:
Imagine you are operating a high-traffic web service with millions of users, and you have decided to distribute their data across, say, ten servers, because no single machine can comfortably hold all of it. The most obvious scheme — the one almost everyone reaches for first — is to hash each user ID and take the result modulo the number of servers. Whichever number falls out tells you which server is responsible for that user's data. As a starting point, this is perfectly reasonable.

The problem reveals itself the first time you try to add an eleventh server. Because the modulus has changed, the mapping from key to server now produces a different answer for nearly every key in the system. The practical consequence is brutal: an enormous fraction of your data has to be moved to a new server simply to accommodate one new machine, and the same disruptive reshuffling occurs whenever a server is removed, fails, or is replaced. In a production system, that kind of churn is, frankly, unacceptable.

Consistent hashing is one of the foundational ideas of modern distributed systems, and it offers an unusually elegant escape from this dilemma. The intuition behind it is more visual than mathematical. You imagine the entire range of possible hash values arranged around the circumference of a circle. Each server is mapped to one or more positions on that circle, and every piece of data is independently hashed onto a position as well. The governing rule is satisfyingly simple: each key belongs to the first server you encounter when moving clockwise from its position on the ring.

The crucial property — the one that justifies the whole construction — is how the arrangement behaves under change. When you add a new server, only the arc immediately preceding its position on the ring changes ownership. Every other key, on every other arc, continues to be served by exactly the same machine as before. When you remove a server, only the data that previously belonged to that one server needs to migrate, and it does so by sliding cleanly to its clockwise neighbor. The vast majority of the dataset remains undisturbed.

That property is precisely why consistent hashing sits at the heart of so many real-world systems — distributed caches, sharded databases, content delivery networks, and modern object stores. It does not eliminate the cost of rebalancing entirely; nothing could. But it confines that cost to the small region of the keyspace that genuinely needs to change, and in doing so, it makes the practical reality of running large distributed systems considerably more humane.

topics.title_ko: [행동면접] 팀과 기술적 결정에서 의견이 갈렸던 경험을 말해보세요

A2.title: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your team on a technical decision.
A2.body: Last year, my team had to choose a database for a new project. Most people wanted to use the same database we always used. I thought a different one was better for our needs. I was worried, but I spoke up in a meeting. I shared my reasons with simple examples. Some people did not agree at first. So I built a small test to show the speed. After they saw the test, they agreed. We changed our plan, and the project worked well. I learned that it is important to speak up, but with proof.

B1.title: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your team on a technical decision.
B1.body:
A few months ago, my team had to choose a database for a new service. Most of my teammates wanted to use the same one we always used, because it felt safe and familiar. I felt strongly that a different database would be a better fit for the kind of data we were storing.

It wasn't easy to disagree, especially with people who had more experience. But instead of just arguing, I built a small test project over a weekend. I showed the team real numbers — query times, memory use, and a clear comparison.

After they saw the results, the conversation changed completely. They started asking practical questions, and in the end we agreed to use the new database. The launch went smoothly, and the service is still running on it.

What I took away is that disagreement is fine — even useful — as long as you bring evidence, not just opinions.

key_phrases.meaning_ko: 의견 차이는 괜찮다, 심지어 유용하다 (em-dash 강조)

B2.title: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your team on a technical decision.
B2.body:
A few months ago, my team had to choose the database for a new internal service. Most of my teammates wanted to stick with the database we always used, mainly because everyone was already comfortable with it. I, on the other hand, was convinced that a different database would handle our access patterns much better, especially as the data grew.

Pushing back wasn't comfortable. A few of my teammates were more senior than me, and I didn't want to come across as inexperienced or arrogant. So I decided that simply repeating my opinion wouldn't be enough.

Over the weekend, I built a small proof-of-concept and ran realistic benchmarks against both options. I prepared a one-page summary with query times, memory usage, and operational trade-offs, and I made sure to acknowledge the downsides of my preferred option as well.

When I shared it in the next meeting, the tone of the discussion shifted. We stopped talking about which database we liked and started talking about which one fit the problem. We ended up going with my recommendation.

The launch went smoothly, and the service has been running on that database for several months with no major issues. What I took away from the experience is that healthy disagreement isn't about winning the argument — it's about replacing opinions with evidence so the team can decide together.

C1.title: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your team on a technical decision.
C1.body:
A few months ago, my team had to choose the database that would back a new internal service. Most of my teammates instinctively wanted to stick with the database we had used for years — partly out of familiarity, and partly because it had already been operationally tuned to our environment. I, however, had become increasingly convinced that a different database would handle our particular access patterns far better, especially as the dataset scaled.

Pushing back wasn't comfortable. Several of my teammates had more experience than I did, and I was conscious of not wanting to come across as inexperienced or, worse, arrogant. So I decided early on that simply restating my opinion would get me nowhere.

Over a weekend, I built a small proof-of-concept and benchmarked both options against workloads that closely resembled what production would look like. I distilled the results into a one-page memo: query latencies, memory footprints, operational trade-offs, and — importantly — an honest account of the weaknesses of my preferred option.

When I shared the document in our next design meeting, the conversation changed almost immediately. We stopped debating which database we liked more and started reasoning about which one actually fit the problem in front of us. By the end of the meeting, we had aligned on my recommendation, and several teammates told me afterwards that the structured comparison had made it much easier to engage with the proposal.

The service launched on schedule and has been running on the new database without serious incident ever since. What I took away from the experience was a lesson I keep returning to: healthy disagreement, especially with more senior colleagues, is rarely won by repeating yourself more loudly. It's won by quietly replacing opinions with evidence and giving the team something concrete enough to push back on.

key_phrases.phrase: was conscious of not wanting to
a lesson I keep returning to
